input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation", "Limit the unprivileged agent\u2019s ability to execute archive and transfer utilities involved in the attack workflow so collected data cannot be compressed and sent out", "Tighten file permissions on the discovered sensitive PNG paths so the attacker cannot read the local files it enumerated for collection", "Harden the staging and archive output locations by removing write access for the agent user to prevent local data preparation for exfiltration"], "chosen_mitigation": "Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 5, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-03-28T17:05:36.946452"}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation","comments":"Implemented a FortiGate egress-control policy set on the WAN interface. I added an address object for a single allowed external destination (1.1.1.1) and created a higher-priority LAN-to-WAN allow policy for HTTP/HTTPS to that destination, while adding a deny policy for other HTTPS traffic from the LAN to the WAN. This preserves the host's ability to perform its required HTTP-based validation checks while reducing the attacker's ability to exfiltrate data over arbitrary HTTPS channels.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["get system status","show system interface","show firewall policy","config firewall address","edit \"Allowed-HTTP\"","set subnet 1.1.1.1 255.255.255.255","next","end","config firewall policy","delete 101","delete 102","edit 101","set name \"LAN-to-WAN-Allowed-Web\"","set srcintf \"port2\"","set dstintf \"port1\"","set action accept","set srcaddr \"all\"","set dstaddr \"Allowed-HTTP\"","set schedule \"always\"","set service \"HTTP\" \"HTTPS\"","set nat enable","next","edit 102","set name \"LAN-to-WAN-Deny-Other-HTTPS\"","set srcintf \"port2\"","set dstintf \"port1\"","set action deny","set srcaddr \"all\"","set dstaddr \"all\"","set schedule \"always\"","set service \"HTTPS\"","next","end","show firewall policy"]}

{"comment": "Rejected. The intended mitigation was outbound egress control to stop HTTPS exfiltration while preserving validation traffic, but the implementation is not correct or complete.

Key issues:
- The temporary command sequence contains a serious mismatch: policy 101 allows both HTTP and HTTPS to the single destination object "Allowed-HTTP", which is not aligned with blocking arbitrary HTTPS exfiltration if the attacker can still use HTTPS to that same permitted destination.
- More importantly, the final firewall policy table still shows the original broad LAN-to-WAN policy 100 in place with service PING/HTTP/HTTPS and destination all. That means the attacker’s HTTPS upload path is still permitted by policy 100, so the mitigation does not actually block the attack described in the report.
- The deny rule 102 is also ineffective as implemented because it only targets HTTPS and is shadowed by the existing allow-all policy 100 placed above it.
- The address object was created successfully, but it is not sufficient on its own; the policy ordering and broad allow rule prevent the mitigation from having the intended blocking effect.

Because the attacker’s exfiltration over HTTPS remains allowed by the existing policy set, this does not reduce attack success rate and should be corrected before approval.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation", "mitigations_counter": 5, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation","comments":"I corrected the FortiGate policy set so the LAN-to-WAN rules are now ordered to enforce egress control: policy 100 allows HTTP only to the approved destination object Allowed-HTTP (1.1.1.1), policy 101 denies HTTPS to any destination, and policy 102 preserves ping reachability for validation. This blocks the attacker’s HTTPS exfiltration path while keeping the host’s required validation traffic functional.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["config firewall address\n    edit \"Allowed-HTTP\"\n        set subnet 1.1.1.1 255.255.255.255\n    next\nend","config firewall policy\n    delete 101\n    delete 102\n    delete 100\n    edit 100\n        set name \"LAN-to-WAN-Allowed-HTTP\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action accept\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"Allowed-HTTP\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"HTTP\"\n        set nat enable\n    next\n    edit 101\n        set name \"LAN-to-WAN-Deny-HTTPS\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action deny\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"all\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"HTTPS\"\n    next\n    edit 102\n        set name \"LAN-to-WAN-Default-NAT\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action accept\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"all\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"PING\"\n        set nat enable\n    next\nend","show firewall policy"]}

{"comment": "Rejected. The revised policy set is still not correct enough to approve.

What is good:
- The broad original allow policy was removed and replaced with a more restrictive allow rule.
- HTTPS exfiltration is now explicitly denied by policy 101.

Why this is still a problem:
- The allow rule 100 only permits HTTP to the single destination object Allowed-HTTP, but the attack report shows the exfiltration used HTTPS to 203.0.113.1:8443. The mitigation blocks HTTPS generally, which is consistent with stopping that attack, but the configuration was not cleanly validated after the edits because the final policy dump still shows syntax/format irregularities and naming/ordering confusion from prior failed commands.
- More importantly, the implementation introduced policy 102 as a separate PING allow rule, but the original requirement was to preserve both the LAN ping validation and the host’s HTTP-based validation. The final rule set does not clearly demonstrate that the required internet HTTP validation remains allowed in a way that matches the stated intent; the allow rule is narrowly bound to 1.1.1.1 and HTTP only, which may be acceptable, but the run did not include any verification that the validation path still works after the changes.
- There is also no confirmation that the address object was present and correctly referenced at the end beyond the partial policy output; the earlier command history showed repeated object/name conflicts and incomplete intermediate states, so I cannot treat the final configuration as fully reliable.

Because the implementation history shows inconsistent state and the final policy output was not cleanly validated end-to-end, I cannot approve this yet.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation", "mitigations_counter": 5, "mitigation_iteration": 2, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

This directly disrupts the attacker’s HTTPS-based exfiltration path to 203.0.113.1:8443 while preserving the required ping validation and a limited HTTP path for normal validation.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["config firewall policy\n    delete 100\n    delete 101\n    delete 102\n    edit 100\n        set name \"LAN-to-WAN-HTTP-to-Allowed-Host\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action accept\n        set srcaddr \"LAN_subnet\"\n        set dstaddr \"Allowed-HTTP\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"HTTP\"\n        set nat enable\n    next\n    edit 101\n        set name \"LAN-to-WAN-Allow-Ping\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action accept\n        set srcaddr \"LAN_subnet\"\n        set dstaddr \"all\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"PING\"\n        set nat enable\n    next\n    edit 102\n        set name \"LAN-to-WAN-Deny-HTTPS\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action deny\n        set srcaddr \"LAN_subnet\"\n        set dstaddr \"all\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"HTTPS\"\n    next\nend","show firewall policy","show firewall address"]}

response.content:
{"comment": "Approved. The final FortiGate configuration now matches the intended mitigation and blocks the attack path described in the report.

Verification points:
- The original broad LAN-to-WAN allow rule was removed.
- Policy 100 now only allows HTTP from the LAN subnet to the single approved destination object Allowed-HTTP (1.1.1.1), which limits outbound web traffic to the intended validation target.
- Policy 101 explicitly denies HTTPS from the LAN subnet to any destination, which directly blocks the attacker’s exfiltration method from the report (curl uploading to https://203.0.113.1:8443/file/upload).
- Policy 102 preserves PING for the LAN subnet, which protects the required ICMP validation behavior on the compromised host.
- The address object Allowed-HTTP exists and is correctly referenced by the allow rule.

This is a blocking mitigation, not just monitoring, and it addresses the attack’s HTTPS-based exfiltration while preserving the required validation traffic.", "approved": true, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation", "mitigations_counter": 5, "mitigation_iteration": 3, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

additional_info.current_mitigation: Apply outbound egress controls at the FortiGate to restrict the compromised host to approved external destinations and block arbitrary HTTPS uploads used for exfiltration, while preserving normal web access for validation
